title: Foundations
lede: The ancient and medieval image-worlds from which later European art repeatedly borrows, argues, and dreams
nav_summary: Power, devotion, monument, ritual, and memory remain tightly bound together.
summary: Power, devotion, monument, empire, ritual, and memory remain tightly bound together in these ancient and medieval image-worlds.
artists: Unknown makers, Phidias workshop, Roman sculptors, Byzantine mosaicists, Insular scribes, cathedral workshops
meta_description: Foundations page of the Art History Field Guide with multiple visual examples and short appreciation notes.

title: Parallel worlds
lede: Major pictorial traditions that unfold beside, beyond, and sometimes in contact with the familiar European storyline
nav_summary: Chinese, Persian, Mughal, Japanese, and South Asian traditions unfold on their own terms.
summary: A wider art history in which Chinese, Persian, Mughal, Japanese, and South Asian traditions unfold according to their own aims and pressures.
artists: Fan Kuan, Sultan Muhammad, Bichitr, Hokusai, Ustad Ahmad Lahori
meta_description: Parallel worlds page of the Art History Field Guide with multiple visual examples and short appreciation notes.

title: Renaissance
lede: A reordering of pictorial space in which observation, proportion, and classical memory become newly deliberate
nav_summary: Space, proportion, and classical memory become newly deliberate.
summary: Space becomes more measured, bodies more deliberate, and the classical past returns as both model and challenge.
artists: Giotto, Jan van Eyck, Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Raphael
meta_description: Renaissance page of the Art History Field Guide with multiple visual examples and short appreciation notes.

title: Baroque and Dutch Golden Age
lede: A world of theatrical light, persuasive movement, private interiors, and public identity
nav_summary: Theatrical light, pressure, spectacle, civic identity, and interior intensity.
summary: The seventeenth century heightens everything: theatrical light, charged gesture, civic identity, and quiet interiors of startling intensity.
artists: Caravaggio, Artemisia Gentileschi, Bernini, Velázquez, Rembrandt, Vermeer
meta_description: Baroque and Dutch Golden Age page of the Art History Field Guide with multiple visual examples and short appreciation notes.

title: 18th and 19th centuries
lede: A century of competing moods in which elegance, virtue, revolution, dream, and ordinary life all demand different pictorial forms
nav_summary: Elegance, virtue, revolution, dream, landscape, and labour all compete for the century's meaning.
summary: This period works less as a sequence than as an argument among elegance, virtue, revolution, dream, landscape, and labour.
artists: Fragonard, Jacques-Louis David, Fuseli, Delacroix, Constable, Millet, Manet
meta_description: 18th and 19th centuries page of the Art History Field Guide with multiple visual examples and short appreciation notes.

title: Impressionism to the modern break
lede: Painting opens itself to weather, sensation, modern life, and the instability of seeing
nav_summary: Weather, sensation, brushwork, and structural instability open the way toward modernism.
summary: Painting admits weather, sensation, visible brushwork, and structural instability, opening gradually into the modern break.
artists: Monet, Morisot, Manet, Cézanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin
meta_description: Impressionism to the modern break page of the Art History Field Guide with multiple visual examples and short appreciation notes.

title: Modernism
lede: A field of restless experiments in which form breaks open and the terms of art are renegotiated
nav_summary: Fracture, reduction, collage, symbol, gesture, and concept renegotiate the terms of art.
summary: A restless field of fracture, reduction, collage, symbol, gesture, and concept in which the terms of art are renegotiated.
artists: Picasso, Kandinsky, Duchamp, Kahlo, Pollock, Warhol
meta_description: Modernism page of the Art History Field Guide with multiple visual examples and short appreciation notes.

title: Contemporary and living questions
lede: A plural present in which media, identity, memory, institutions, and circulation all shape the work
nav_summary: Plural, global, and contested: media, identity, institutions, memory, and circulation all shape the encounter.
summary: A plural present in which media, identity, institutions, memory, and circulation all shape the encounter with the work.
artists: Judy Chicago, Cindy Sherman, Louise Bourgeois, and many more living contexts
meta_description: Contemporary and living questions page of the Art History Field Guide with multiple visual examples and short appreciation notes.
